attributes.stem:
A 71-year-old woman falls and receives a blow to the left side of the head. Later that day she reports double vision when looking to left.

The photograph shows the woman's eyes when she is trying to look to her left hand side (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely explanation for her symptoms?
attributes.explanation:
This question requires a good understanding of the function of the cranial nerves, in particular three, four and six that control eye movements, and how they may be affected in a palsy.

The third cranial nerve (oculomotor) innervates the sphincter pupillae and ciliary muscles with parasympathetic supply (constriction), the levator palpebrae (elevation of the eyelid) and the the majority of the extra-ocular eye muscles (superior rectus, inferior rectus, medial rectus and inferior oblique). Palsy of this nerve creates the "down and out" looking eye, which is not demonstrated by this image. Causes of CN3 palsy include compression (aneurysm, herniation) or ischaemia (diabetes, hypertension).

The fourth cranial nerve (trochlear nerve) innervates the superior oblique, which turns the eye inward and down. A palsy affecting this nerve results in the eye being unable to perform these movements, making actions such as climbing stairs difficult as this requires the eye to invert and look down. Causes of this include serious headstrikes, such as those in motor vehicle accidents.

The sixth cranial nerve (abducens) innervates the lateral rectus muscle, which functions to abduct the eye. The image in the stem above demonstrates that the left eye is unable to abduct, as the person is attempting to look to the left side, the right eye is adducted, but the left eye remains straight and cannot abduct. There are many causes of a palsy affecting this nerve including head trauma, aneurysm, ischaemia etc.
learningPoints: The cranial nerves that control eye movements are three, four and six
CN3 palsy results in "down and out" appearance of the eye
CN6 palsy prevents the eye from abducting
references: Rubin, M. Third Cranial Nerve (Oculomotor Nerve) Palsy. 2020. MSD Manuals. Access at: https://www.msdmanuals.com/en-au/home/brain,-spinal-cord,-and-nerve-disorders/cranial-nerve-disorders/third-cranial-nerve-oculomotor-nerve-palsy.

Joyce C, Le PH, Peterson DC. Neuroanatomy, Cranial Nerve 3 (Oculomotor) [Updated 2020 Jul 27]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2021 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK537126/

Nguyen V, Reddy V, Varacallo M. Neuroanatomy, Cranial Nerve 6 (Abducens) [Updated 2020 Nov 19]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2021 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430711/

Rubin, M. Sixth Cranial (Abducens) Nerve Palsy. 2020. MSD Manuals. Access at: https://www.msdmanuals.com/en-au/professional/neurologic-disorders/neuro-ophthalmologic-and-cranial-nerve-disorders/sixth-cranial-abducens-nerve-palsy.
